questions.question: Which findings are signs of compensated shock?
options: A. Hypotension and bradycardia
B. Pale skin, tachycardia, and tachypnea
C. Normal skin color and normal heart rate
D. Bright red skin and bradypnea
questions.rationale: Compensated shock presents with pale skin, tachycardia, and tachypnea — the body is compensating and blood pressure may still be normal.

questions.question: What does a falling blood pressure indicate in a shock patient?
options: A. Compensated shock
B. Decompensated (progressive) shock
C. Full recovery
D. Nothing significant
questions.rationale: Falling blood pressure marks decompensated shock, the stage where compensatory mechanisms have failed to maintain perfusion pressure.

questions.question: Why should shock patients be kept warm?
options: A. For comfort only, with no physiologic benefit
B. Hypothermia worsens coagulopathy and impairs cardiac function, compounding shock
C. Warmth increases blood loss
D. It has no effect on outcome
questions.rationale: Covering shock patients with a blanket prevents heat loss, which otherwise worsens coagulopathy and cardiac function — this is a physiologic intervention, not just comfort.

questions.question: When are inotropic vasopressors like dopamine indicated?
options: A. All types of shock equally
B. Persistent hypotension in cardiogenic shock
C. Only in hypovolemic shock
D. Only in anaphylaxis
questions.rationale: Inotropic vasopressors are indicated for persistent hypotension specifically in cardiogenic shock, where the heart's pumping ability is the underlying problem.

questions.question: Why is blood pressure considered a 'late' indicator of shock?
options: A. It changes before any other vital sign
B. Compensatory mechanisms maintain BP initially, so by the time it falls the patient has already been in shock
C. Blood pressure never changes in shock
D. It is the first and most sensitive sign of shock
questions.rationale: Because tachycardia and vasoconstriction compensate early, blood pressure remains normal until compensation fails — making it a late, not early, marker.

questions.question: A trauma patient is pale, tachycardic, and tachypneic, but their blood pressure is 118/76. What is the correct interpretation?
options: A. No shock is present because BP is normal
B. Compensated shock is likely present despite the normal blood pressure
C. This patient is stable and needs no further monitoring
D. These findings are unrelated to shock
questions.rationale: Pale skin, tachycardia, and tachypnea with a still-normal BP are the classic presentation of compensated shock — treatment should not wait for hypotension to develop.

questions.question: A patient in cardiogenic shock is given a large fluid bolus similar to what a hypovolemic patient would receive. What is the risk?
options: A. No risk — fluids are always beneficial in shock
B. Worsening pulmonary edema, since the heart cannot pump the extra volume effectively
C. Immediate improvement in all cases
D. Fluids have no effect on a failing heart
questions.rationale: In cardiogenic shock, the pump itself has failed, so large fluid volumes can precipitate or worsen pulmonary edema rather than improve perfusion.

questions.question: Which shock category is suggested by fever and a likely infectious source?
options: A. Hypovolemic
B. Distributive (septic)
C. Obstructive
D. Cardiogenic
questions.rationale: Fever with an infectious source points toward septic shock, a distributive shock category caused by pathologic vasodilation.

questions.question: Which shock category requires mechanical correction (e.g., decompression) rather than primarily fluids or vasopressors?
options: A. Hypovolemic
B. Obstructive (e.g., tension pneumothorax, tamponade)
C. Septic
D. Anaphylactic
questions.rationale: Obstructive shock is caused by a mechanical problem (tamponade, tension pneumothorax) that requires a mechanical fix — fluids and vasopressors alone will not correct the underlying obstruction.

questions.question: What accompanies the falling blood pressure of decompensated shock?
options: A. Improved mental status
B. Altered mental status from inadequate cerebral perfusion
C. No change in mentation
D. Increased urine output
questions.rationale: As perfusion pressure falls in decompensated shock, cerebral perfusion suffers, producing altered mental status alongside the hypotension.

questions.question: Why is it important to identify the SPECIFIC category of shock rather than treating 'shock' generically?
options: A. All shock categories are treated identically
B. Fluids, vasopressors, epinephrine, and mechanical correction are not interchangeable — matching treatment to category matters
C. Category identification is only for documentation
D. Treatment never depends on the cause
questions.rationale: Each shock category has a different primary intervention; using the wrong one (e.g., large fluid boluses in cardiogenic shock) can worsen the patient's condition.

questions.question: A patient in shock is left uncovered on a cold ambulance floor during a long transport. What physiologic consequence is most likely?
options: A. Improved cardiac function
B. Worsening coagulopathy and cardiac function from hypothermia, compounding the shock state
C. No effect on outcome
D. Faster recovery
questions.rationale: Allowing a shock patient to become hypothermic worsens coagulopathy and cardiac performance — keeping the patient warm is an active treatment step, not just comfort.

questions.question: Which statement about compensated vs decompensated shock is TRUE?
options: A. Decompensated shock always precedes compensated shock
B. Compensated shock precedes decompensated shock as compensatory mechanisms progressively fail
C. They are unrelated stages
D. Blood pressure falls first in compensated shock
questions.rationale: Shock progresses from compensated (BP maintained, subtle signs present) to decompensated (BP falls, mental status affected) as the body's compensatory mechanisms are overwhelmed.

questions.question: A patient with known heart failure develops hypotension, cool extremities, and pulmonary crackles. Which shock category and initial management approach fit best?
options: A. Hypovolemic shock — give a large crystalloid bolus
B. Cardiogenic shock — consider inotropic/vasopressor support and cautious fluid administration
C. Anaphylactic shock — give epinephrine
D. Obstructive shock — needle decompression
questions.rationale: Heart failure history with hypotension and pulmonary crackles suggests cardiogenic shock (pump failure), where inotropic/vasopressor support is favored over aggressive fluid boluses, which risk worsening pulmonary edema.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of distributive shock?
options: A. Bleeding from a laceration
B. Anaphylaxis causing widespread vasodilation
C. Myocardial infarction with pump failure
D. Tension pneumothorax
questions.rationale: Anaphylaxis causes distributive shock through pathologic vasodilation, distinct from hypovolemic (bleeding), cardiogenic (MI), or obstructive (tension pneumothorax) shock.
